era_context: The Qin Dynasty has just collapsed (210 BCE), and China enters a period of civil war and fragmentation. Liu Bang (future Emperor Gaozu) and Xiang Yu (the Hegemon-King of Western Chu) are the dominant warlords competing for control. In the Mediterranean, the Second Punic War continues; Scipio Africanus is consolidating Roman power in Spain. The Seleucid and Ptolemaic empires remain the dominant powers in the Hellenistic East.

events.title: Liu Bang proclaimed King of Han
events.description: Liu Bang is proclaimed King of Han by the warlord coalition that has overthrown the Qin Dynasty. This marks a crucial step in Liu Bang's rise to power. He receives the Han region (in modern Shaanxi) as his domain, a relatively modest territory compared to the holdings of rivals like Xiang Yu. However, Liu Bang uses this base to build a strong administrative and military apparatus. The title 'King of Han' is the foundation of what will become the Han Dynasty.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, compiled ~111 CE

events.certainty_note: The broad outline is secure, but the precise date within 206 BCE is uncertain. The Shiji provides the primary narrative, but it was compiled ~100 years after these events. Some sources place this event in 207 BCE.
cross_references: Leads to the Chu-Han Contention (206–202 BCE)

events.title: Xiang Yu proclaimed Hegemon-King of Western Chu
events.description: Xiang Yu, a powerful warlord and general, is proclaimed Hegemon-King (Ba Wang) of Western Chu by the coalition of anti-Qin forces. Xiang Yu receives the largest and most powerful territory in the post-Qin fragmentation, including much of the Yangtze River valley and central China. His rise to prominence is marked by military victories and his reputation as a formidable warrior. However, his autocratic style and harsh governance alienate many allies, setting the stage for conflict with Liu Bang.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE

events.certainty_note: The event is well-attested in the Shiji, but precise dating within 206 BCE is uncertain. The Shiji's account of Xiang Yu is known to be colored by Han-era bias against the defeated rival.
cross_references: Leads to the Chu-Han Contention (206–202 BCE)

events.title: Scipio Africanus captures Gades (Cadiz)
events.description: Scipio Africanus captures the city of Gades (modern Cadiz) in southwestern Spain, one of the last major Carthaginian strongholds in Iberia. This victory effectively ends Carthaginian control of the Iberian peninsula and secures Roman dominance in the region. The capture of Gades is a major strategic victory for Rome, as it eliminates Carthage's primary source of wealth and military recruitment in the western Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Gades is well-documented in Roman sources. The date 206 BCE is secure.
cross_references: Part of the Second Punic War (218–201 BCE)

events.title: Qin Dynasty officially ends
events.description: The Qin Dynasty, which unified China in 221 BCE under Qin Shi Huang, formally collapses. The last Qin ruler, Qin Er Shi (the Second Emperor), is deposed and executed. The Qin's harsh legalist policies, heavy taxation, and massive construction projects (including the Great Wall and the Terracotta Army) had provoked widespread rebellion. The collapse of the Qin marks the end of the first unified Chinese empire and the beginning of the Chu-Han Contention, a period of civil war that will last until 202 BCE.
key_figures: Qin Er Shi (Second Emperor of Qin, deposed)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE

events.certainty_note: The collapse of the Qin Dynasty is well-established. The date 206 BCE is the conventional date for the end of the Qin, though some sources place the final collapse slightly later.
cross_references: Leads to the Chu-Han Contention (206–202 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Shiji's account of the Qin collapse and the rise of Liu Bang and Xiang Yu is known to contain Han-era propaganda favorable to Liu Bang and hostile to Xiang Yu. Modern scholarship has questioned some of the Shiji's characterizations of Xiang Yu as purely tyrannical; some evidence suggests his governance was more complex. Additionally, the precise chronology of events in 206 BCE is uncertain, and some sources place key events (such as Liu Bang's proclamation as King of Han) in 207 BCE instead.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 206 BCE is dominated by Chinese sources (Shiji, Han Shu), which provide detailed narratives but were compiled decades to centuries after the events. These sources reflect Han-era perspectives and contain clear bias against the defeated Xiang Yu. The Mediterranean sources (Livy, Polybius) are contemporary or near-contemporary but focus on the Second Punic War and provide no information about China. The collapse of the Qin is one of the most significant events of this year globally, but it is known almost entirely through later Chinese compilations.

graph_edges.note: Liu Bang's establishment as King of Han leads to his victory in the Chu-Han Contention
Xiang Yu's rise as Hegemon-King leads to conflict with Liu Bang
The collapse of the Qin Dynasty enables Liu Bang's rise
Scipio's capture of Gades is part of the Second Punic War
